I noticed that that this PR is adding some checks for `#if OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_{NAME}` in some of the OT public header and/or platform header (e.g., `radio.h`).

This is generally avoided with OT, i.e. we don't include such `#if` checks in any of public or platform headers. Note that none of the public or platform header file include the `"openthread-core-config.h"` (see below for more background/reason for this). 

Here is the common aproach we use in public/platform headers:
- In public OT API, we document if an entire set of APIs or a specific OT API requires certain config option. There are many examples of this. 
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/f4b188d79dafdbde081497fcd116fa843fb2be37/include/openthread/dataset_updater.h#L49-L52

- In the case of types (`struct`, `enum`, callback, etc) definitions in public/platform header, the types are generally included without any `#if` check and it is documented if/how their use is tied to some config option
https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/f4b188d79dafdbde081497fcd116fa843fb2be37/include/openthread/link.h#L61-L75

I have not had the chance to look in detail in this PR and PSA implementation, but wanted to point this out so we can consider how to address this.

-----

This older issue https://github.com/openthread/openthread/issues/4333 points to some background on why we aim to avoid use of `#if` config check in the public/platform headers. It mainly boils down to how OT can be integrated in projects and products. 
- OT can be built as a library which is then linked with other modules (application layer, platform implementation, OS, etc.) to form the device's firmware or an executable.
   - The OT public (and platform) header file are exposed to other modules and can be included in other modules so that they can use OT APIs. 
- The `OPENTRHEAD_CONFIG` are intended for project integrator to configure OT library itself. 
  - While some of the bigger features can be configured (enabled) through cmake or autoconf build config options, there are many configs (paramters of smaller feature) that are defined in a config header file.
- The project integrator can provide a project specific OT config header file (through the OPENTHREAD_PROJECT_CORE_CONFIG_FILE or cmake `OT_CONIFG`) when build OT library.
- In such a case, such a project-specific header file is not owned by OT code and therefore we cannot expose it to other libraries as a public header file.

on_request is called first when a request arrives. authorize_request is a helper for subclasses to use, so they don't have to handle the credential or cached token ([for example](https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/ddaf7529f8fb35c7888e5eeded1e0e0f956709cd/sdk/core/azure-mgmt-core/azure/mgmt/core/policies/_authentication.py#L51)). Assuming neither method is overridden, authorize_request would overwrite a token applied by on_request. Which is fine, the policy is designed for a scenario like this:
1. on_request authorizes the request
2. policy sends the request to the RP
3. RP responds with a challenge
4. on_challenge authorizes the request with a new token (perhaps by calling authorize_request)
5. policy sends the request to the RP again

As far as I can tell, there are two codepaths that end up calling `renewal.should_renew()`. The first *is* called after an `_init_le_client` call inside `main.run()` or `main.certonly()`, but would require passing that client from there all the way through
- `_find_cert()`
- `_find_lineage_for_domains_and_certname`
- `_find_lineage_for_domains`
- `_handle_identical_cert_request`
- finally to `renewal.should_renew`.

The second codepath is `main.renew()` --> `renewal.handle_renewal_request()` --> `renewal.should_renew()`, and that code path does *not* call `_init_le_client` prior to reaching this code, and in fact does the same unfortunate "import `main` inside a function so it isn't technically an import cycle" dance there:
https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/d2578e05e7cfd6e408dc45b6e48d0c42685c014d/certbot/certbot/_internal/renewal.py#L458-L475

Due to the first codepath requiring additional arguments to a lot of functions, and the second codepath not having access to a client at all, I went with this for now. I'd really love to not land this version though 🤣

`aBytesRemaining` counts the number of free bytes in the receive buffer, not including any OOO segments.

I added `aBytesRemaining` based on your feedback from Monday's meeting; the application also needs some feedback if the receive buffer is full and some data needs to be consumed before more data will be added to the receive buffer. This code does not register after fork hook in the master process, it only does in the children process. Which is correct, since it's possible for a uwsgi child process to call `os.fork`.

If the user enables `py-call-osafterfork` then the problem also does not arise: the `start` method does not register `forksafe` hook in the master process, it just returns right away and wait for the uwsgi postfork hook to be triggered – so we're still fine.

We may want to offer the use of setting the network key as a literal even with PSA. As this will mimic the real products better. In real products often the network key is either generated or derived from TLVs and in both cases the stack has to deal with literal keys and not key refs. Also asking the user set the network key using key ref would mean the user having to access the platform code to import the key himself, or calling the PSA APIs in application. This would create complications when we move the code to secure world, as now you will have to deal with App accessing PSA APIs as well.

I don't see the app ever needing to get the key refs, especially is the the platform APIs to export the key is not available and the key itself is not exportable. 

So I would propose keeping the getters and setters to work only on literal keys and modify these to abstract the key references where-ever necessary. As such I am inclined towards the first approach of adding `mKeyType` to network key and PSKc.

My apologies, I think there is a misunderstanding because I did not interpretate correctly your intention in this PR. I was not thinking about migrating any tests from Travis to Azure, but just to write the specific logic of building, testing and deploying snaps into Azure instead of Travis because of the various reasons described in this PR comments.

But in my mind testing snaps was about launching integration tests only (so the `integration-external` tox target you added). Similarly to what was done in Robie repo originally.

So again in my request was concerning only the small incremental change done by this PR, which was then to "just" add a continuous delivery of snaps to the edge channel after a single test, and not imply to rethink right now the all CI system.

However I see know that your intention is to wait for all tests existing in Travis to succeed before pushing the snap to edge. I understand now more the reticence you have, and the trade your are doing between accepting a suboptimal Travis pipeline for snaps build versus a major refactoring of the CI system in Certbot.

Finally it comes all up to that question: is it really needed to wait for all tests to be run before pushing the snap? In my opinion, this is not necessary, and the `integration-external` is sufficient, for the following reasons:
- several tests (basically almost all from the extended tests suite) are not concerning snaps, either because they are testing a specific Python version, or testing Certbot installed bare on specific systems
- remaining tests that are testing the internal logic of Certbot are already executed continuously on master, either through PR integrations, or in nightly builds, giving us time to fix things before release time
- outcome of snap build would be for the edge channel, which I think is acceptable to do with limited blocking tests, in particular considering that tests that would fail at the time the snap is pushed and that we would not have the opportunity to see failing before would be from the extended test suite, which is not concerning the snap is I said before
- pushing the snap to beta is a manual action, giving the releaser the opportunity to review all outcomes of the release branch (including the extended test suite from Travis), and not give his sign off if there is any doubt

I sounds to me a quite agile workflow, with shorter multiple pipelines for different purposes even allowing "live" testing thanks to the edge channel if weird things are detected in the tests.

So it is now a trade-off between a suboptimal Travis pipeline for snaps versus not having the extended test suite blocking the push to edge channel because it is done in a dedicated pipeline in Travis. If you still think we really need to have all tests run before pushing to edge channel, I am totally agree with you that it does not worth to rethink the CI system right now. Otherwise, well I think it really makes sense to have the specific snap build/integration-test/deploy in Azure Pipeline.

In a nutshell: the scipy default are not sufficient. If you pass not jacobian/hessian information to trust-constr it will blow up into your face saying it needs both.

In my view you should be able to use any of our wrapped minimizers with any kind of model, even those for which we don't know a jacobian/hessian analytically. That means that in those cases you absolutely must use a gradient/hessian based minimizer you need to be able to provide a jacobian/hessian. In that case the responsibility is on the user to 1) make sure they actually do, and 2) whatever they pass conforms to the API and 3) produces the correct values though.

In any case, if the user thinks they know better than what we provide for them, we should honor that since they have more intimate knowledge/information about their system/model. It's up to them to analyze the ashes once it blows up though.

So to wrap up, this is not an issue in 95% of the cases where the sane symfit determined analytical jacobian/hessian are passed. Allowing users to override our defaults enables them to also use this is the other 5% of the cases though.

* `get()`
* `insert()`
* `insert_batch()`
* `begin()`
* `commit()`

The reason to use a static method to do all of the work is to make it easier to test different aspects of the codebase.  In particular, the instance methods track `open_chain_tip` in order to determine whether or not the caller correctly called `.begin()` before `.insert()` or `.insert_batch()`, and then correctly called `.commit()` to materialize the changes.

Right, for something that remains compatible with the existing API I think the options here are...

* A custom `Headers` sequence, that exposes the extra information in a `.raw_items()` interface or similar, that returns a two-tuple of `(case-sensitive-name, value)` for usages that require the raw casing info.
* A custom `Headers` sequence, the returns `Header` instances, that can iterate as two-tuples, but also expose `.name`, `.case_sensitive_name` and `.value` attributes, which are available for usages that require the raw casing info.

Or some variation on those. (Eg. this PR which currently has `.raw()` returning the three-tuple of info.)
Personally I'm fairly agnostic, as both the above options seem reasonable enough. The `Header` case has the most extra overhead, since it creates and accesses a per-header instance rather than the plain tuple, while I wouldn't expect the `.raw_items()` approach to introduce anything really noticeable, but I could run through some timings on each of the options to help better inform our options.

There is a library `pytz` that has some useful methods to retrieve timezones by country.

For US the list is the following:
```
America/New_York
America/Detroit
America/Kentucky/Louisville
America/Kentucky/Monticello
America/Indiana/Indianapolis
America/Indiana/Vincennes
America/Indiana/Winamac
America/Indiana/Marengo
America/Indiana/Petersburg
America/Indiana/Vevay
America/Chicago
America/Indiana/Tell_City
America/Indiana/Knox
America/Menominee
America/North_Dakota/Center
America/North_Dakota/New_Salem
America/North_Dakota/Beulah
America/Denver
America/Boise
America/Phoenix
America/Los_Angeles
America/Anchorage
America/Juneau
America/Sitka
America/Metlakatla
America/Yakutat
America/Nome
America/Adak
Pacific/Honolulu
```
However, some timezones are not included like:
Pacific/Guam
Pacific/Samoa
America/Puerto_Rico

In addition, a mapping between these timezones and their code is needed like `EST -> America/New_York`

So that since we are already using `dateutil` I think this is the simpler way to handle timezones.

I've added the timezones above to the list:

```
"CHST":"Pacific/Guam"
"SST": "Pacific/Samoa"
"AST": "America/Puerto_Rico"
```
So according to [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_in_the_United_States) now we have all the US timezones.

But I'm afraid that we might need an additional mapping between time zone codes and courts. I was checking court timezones in CL and I'm surprised that some courts use a local timezone e.g: `"arb": "US/Arizona"`, while Arizona uses MST time, which I mapped as `America/Denver`, so not sure if some of these courts use a different timezone according if some regions of the state use DST or not, do you know if the reason is that?

So far I've found that only appellate courts use local timezones and district courts use GMT, so maybe we don't need to map all the timezone names, I'll confirm this by checking RSS for all district/bankruptcy courts.

for batch in ds.iter_batches():

You do use `carry`... Oh! I was confused because I thought that you were calculating new `nextstarts` and `nextstops`... But you are? Or you're not using them? The kernel you want should take the old `starts` and `stops` in (which means the base pointers, offsets for both, but only the length of `starts` because excess values in `stops` are ignored) and it should write a `nextcarry` out (you named it `toarray`). Conventionally, I've been putting the arrays that get written _first_ in the argument list. For C functions, I need conventions like that to stay sane.

Ignoring the `starts_.offset()` and `stops_.offset()` is very likely your error. But you also need to precalculate the length with a second kernel. There's no way to guess how big it can be from the `starts` length and the `content_` length: the fact that it can have gaps _and_ overlaps allows it to be arbitrarily big.

Sorry for the late reply. Yeah, there is definitely a place for both patterns (raising early / collating errors as a group and raising all at once).

I would tend to agree with Eric's general intuition (using "code runtime" as a rough gauge); For example, import errors are typically collated together (e.g. this function requires optional import `xxx`, `yyy` and `zzz`, please pip install them. [Pandas](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/main/pandas/__init__.py#L17) does this.). You can imagine how annoying it might be if this information gets relayed to you one package at a time.

In our particular case, we're essentially checking if there is an intersection between the two sets of names `index` and `exclude` (which was how the code was written before this PR). Not particularly computationally expensive I think, if that's the primary worry here.
The only instance you'll gain some time advantage is when there *is* an intersection (ala, raising the error early), but the trade-off in terms of user experience is too huge, IMO. 

Not to say there aren't situations where raising early is useful -- there are --; just not in this particular case, I think.

Current DNS Client implementation facilitates DNS cache poisoning attacks by implementing deterministic way of assigning IDs (just increment ID by one).

If ID is generated with poor Pseudo-Random Number Generator, attacker has higher probability of success. While i personally didn't do any calculation with use of our PRNG, there are few papers in the internet suggesting to use TRNG mechanism.

`Crypto` RNG module is used without error checking in few places in OpenThread, in other places assertion like here is implemented. I would say, that we can improve it in subsequent PR, for example by implementing a backup mechanism to use `NonCrypto` module in case `Crypto` fails. Hey Rich, I just spent a significant amount of time in the WKT2 docs, PROJ 6 docs, and GDAL 3 Docs. I _think_ I'm starting to develop a deeper understanding. I think the basics are as follows:

- GDAL 3 conforms to the authority defining coordinate reference systems (CRS). 
- For Geographic CRS (WGS84), this means no longer ignoring Axis Order but abiding by LAT,LON order
- For Projected CRS (UTM), most EPSG in the registry use Easting, Northing order
- We think of things in general as **x**,**y** because it is convenient, but now a `Transform` expects Axis Order, where  if `long=x` and `lat=y`, GDAL 3 would take `Transform(y, x)`
- Thus when transforming from Projected CRS to Geographic CRS, `Transform(x,y)` will return `y,x` (Lat,Lon)
- Thus when transforming from Geographic CRS to Projected CRS, `Transform(y,x)` will return `x,y` (Easting, Northing)
- Thus when transforming from Geographic CRS to Geographic CRS, `Transform(y,x)` will return `y,x` (Lat,Lon)
- Thus when transforming from Projected CRS to Projected CRS, `Transform(x,y)` will return `x,y` (Easting, Northing)

**note:** Most projected CRS use Easting first, Northing second convention, however, some defined in the EPSG registry use the reverse convention.

So, I think you're correct in that `not.____.IsProjected()` is probably wrong, but `_____.IsGeographic()` _could_ be OK. Basically that Axis Order setting `SetAxisMappingStrategy(osr.OAMS_TRADITIONAL_GIS_ORDER)` is saying, if we've got a Geographic CRS, use Lon,Lat like the good old days (since we think of `lon,lat` as `x,y`. We certainly can, but maybe should, **NOT** do that. 

Perhaps a better solution is to create a mapping of Axis Order to Geographic (Lat,Lon) and Projected (Easting, Northing), such that when handling the extents and bounding box points we can correctly return _x_, _y_.

You can't get the size of the frame (which is the total number of members to be converted) until you have constructed a frame, but once you construct the frame the items are selected.  It does autogrow the frame if you exceed.

In this case each member, field, and array access is generating one reference.  For some of my test cases I was hitting 256 which produced a warning, thus I took it to the next power of 2. The Ada generator produces:

```Ada
C := Skip_Utf8_Backward (Z, -5);
```

where the C generator produces:

```
int ret = skip_utf8(z->p, z->c, z->lb, z->l, - -5);
```

Indeed the `skip_utf8` should accept only positive values. It does not make sense to pass negative values to that function. The Ada compiler detected the error at compilation time:

```
stemmer-yiddish.adb:489:26: warning: value not in range of type "Standard.Positive"
stemmer-yiddish.adb:489:26: warning: "Constraint_Error" will be raised at run time
```

And then it fails at runtime as expected.

I'll have a look at the Ada code generator to check why the negation is dropped.

So right now this test class is a subclass of the standard library's `unittest.TestCase` (or whatever it is) class. We run our tests with `pytest` now so we don't *need* to use `unittest` and can create regular functions (ex. `def test_X`) or test classes (ex. `TestSomething`) with test methods (ex. `def test_something_basic(self)`). When we don't use unittest classes we can use more functionality provided by pytest like the parametrize decorator: https://docs.pytest.org/en/6.2.x/parametrize.html

So if you moved this method outside the unittest class, you could do:

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize(input_file,
[
    "GIIBUFRProduct_20191106130000Z_00_OMPEFS04_MET11_FES_E0000",
    ... other filenames ...,
])
def test_seviri_l2_bufr(self, input_file):
    seviri_l2_bufr_test(input_file)
```

I realize though that the `sevirir_l2_bufr_test` is a method on the unittest class so that would have to be extracted and changed to use regular assert statements (ex. `assert X == Y` instead of `self.assertEqual(X, Y)`). ```yaml
# Define procedural code to handle the response.
response:
- loop:
    collection: $resp.entities
    variable:   entity
    body:
    - comment: ["Access value by key:"]
    - print:
      - "URL: %s"
      - entity.metadata{"wikipedia_url"}

    - comment: ["Loop over keys and values:"]
    - loop:
        map: entity.metadata
        key: key
        value: value
        body:
        - print: ["%s: %s", key, value]

    - comment: ["Loop over just keys:"]
    - loop:
        map: entity.metadata
        key: the_key
        body:
        - print: ["Key: %s", the_key]

    - comment: ["Loop over just values:"]
    - loop:
        map: entity.metadata
        value: the_value
        body:
        - print: ["Value: %s", the_value]
```

We rely on the implicit behavior of `make_zip` to clean up for us:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/f176fb0dfc302a4cc58c374e59d2af6898629a33/test/datasets_utils.py#L887-L891

It will remove all files and folders that were added to the archive. By specifying `root / "GTSRB" / "Training"` as source for the files and folders to add, the folder `GTSRB` is not part of the removal. Thus, after the creation of the archive, an empty `GTSRB` folder is left in the root directory. This trips the new check.

By specifying `root / "GTSRB"` the folder is part of the removal schedule and we don't have to do it manually like

make_zip(root, "GTSRB-Training_fixed.zip", root / "GTSRB" / "Training" )
(root / "GTSRB").rmdir()
```

I went ahead and just exposed the pipeline object (in line with @YarShev 's first suggestion). 

To use the Pipeline API, import the `PandasQueryPipeline` from `modin.experimental.batch.pipeline` and initialize it with a dataframe, an axis to pipeline over, and an optional number of partitions (defaults to `NPartitions.get`. Then, you can add queries using the Pipeline object's `add_query` method, change the dataframe to pipeline by using the `update_df` method, and compute the results using the `compute_batch` method. 

E.g.:
```python
import modin.pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from modin.experimental.batch.pipeline import PandasQueryPipeline
df = pd.DataFrame(np.random.randint(0, 1, (1000, 1000))
pipeline = PandasQueryPipeline(df, 0, num_partitions=20)
pipeline.add_query(lambda df: df * -3, is_output=True)
pipeline.update_df(pd.DataFrame([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]]))
outputs = pipeline.compute_batch()
```

Yes, this should be safe and more efficient sine we make use of the default redis connection pool, in other to reduce the overhead of creating/destroying connections constantly.

From redis-py documenation about [Connection Pools](https://github.com/redis/redis-py#connection-pools):
```
Behind the scenes, redis-py uses a connection pool to manage connections to a Redis server. By default, each Redis instance you create will in turn create its own connection pool.
```

Before, the code created a new redis client instance for each request with its associated connection pool, but then it did not make any use of the connection pool associated to the instance, because when a new connection came instead of reusing it will create a new instance.

For that reason, just moving the creation of the redis client instance to the contructor will make the trick (similarly to how it would be done for other databases).

To me, it seems the purpose of `original_nouns` is to detect whether the help is loaded for the original command like `vm` or its children, like `vm availability-set`, so that we only load AI examples for the command itself, not its children.

```
> az vm -h

Group
    az vm              << Load AI examples for it

Subgroups:
    availability-set   << Don't load AI examples for these
    boot-diagnostics
    diagnostics
...
```
If so, an easier approach is to copy the logic from `knack.help.CLIHelp.show_help` to `azure.cli.core._help.AzCliHelp.show_help` and add the replacement/addition logic here, instead of going very deep and evaluate for every `CliHelpFile.load` whether the help is retrieved for the original command - `vm`. In other words, we can actively add the AI examples after the help is rendered, instead of passively evaluating the given command.

```py
    # azure.cli.core._help.AzCliHelp.show_help
    def show_help(self, cli_name, nouns, parser, is_group):
        self.update_loaders_with_help_file_contents(nouns)
        self.original_nouns = ' '.join(nouns)
        import colorama
        colorama.init(autoreset=True)
        delimiters = ' '.join(nouns)
        help_file = self.command_help_cls(self, delimiters, parser) if not is_group \
            else self.group_help_cls(self, delimiters, parser)
        help_file.load(parser)
        if not nouns:
            help_file.command = ''
        else:
            new_examples = self.check_for_new_examples(help_file.command)  # ai examples are added here
            if new_examples:
                help_file.examples = new_examples
        self._print_detailed_help(cli_name, help_file)

        print(SURVEY_PROMPT)
```

This is a bit different from the repr changes that follow on from #1966 style repr tidyups:

repr on this class is still the default, which gives something like this (on Python 3.11):

```
>>> repr(p.JobState.COMPLETED)
'<JobState.COMPLETED: 4>'
```

but this `__str__` was addred in PR #2653 specifically because the str behaviour changed from Python 3.10 to Python 3.11 to rendering an IntEnum as the integer value, rather than as the name of the state.

This makes sense for the use-case it was intended for (when tightening an already existing `int` field into an `IntEnum`) but in the Parsl case, I wanted to preserve the loose human representation being the state name (which is the case in Python <=3.10)

For example, when used in log messages, it is nicer to represent the state to a human reader as `"COMPLETED"` rather than as `4`.

That is an unfortunate chicken and egg problem. I think the root cause is that the scripts aren't decoupled from each other.

There are two scripts:

1. prepare_python_system_tests.py
2. run_python_system_tests.py

The first one prepares for, then runs the selected system test. This is not what the name of the script conveys. It calls on the second script in order to do it.

What if you decoupled the two scripts so that they are more specific and do just the one thing?

The first script prepares to run tests, the second one runs the specified test. It means the test suites need to call two scripts, but it also means that you could in theory test several drivers (for example, NI-SCOPE and NI-TClk) on the same Test Suite which would be desirable.

Correct. I thought I mentioned this somewhere, but maybe I didn't. It looks like I only complained about the inconsistent and proprietary on-disk and in-memory data structure, which is the meat of SSlibSigner. I just couldn't find a way to fix it, without breaking the SSlibSigner interface.

So, yes, I do consider CryptoSigner to be a modern replacement for SSlibSigner use cases. And for a transition period, CryptoSigner can also provide the implementation for SSlibSigner, which would allow us to deprecate `key*` independently of SSlibSigner. I'm okay to revert the latter part, if that only makes things more complicated.

Regarding class hierarchy of CryptoSigner, I agree that the subclasses could be non-public API. The only reason why I made them public, is to namespace the corresponding generate methods: `RSASigner.generate`, etc. But we could also expose these directly on CryptoSigner, e.g. `CryptoSigner.generate_rsa`, etc.

There is a rule of thumb that you shouldn't use bare `except:`, but, that rule is assuming a specific context, that doesn't apply here :-).

Often, programmers want to say "try to do this, and if it fails, I don't care why, try something else". Like you might try the operation again, or try doing it another way. And in this case new Python programmers often write `except:`, because they think it means "I don't care why it failed, just that it failed". But, that's not really what bare `except:` does! The problem is that there are some special exceptions, like `KeyboardInterrupt`, that don't mean the thing you were trying failed, they mean something else – like for `KeyboardInterrupt`, it means "the user hit control-C and wants your program to exit". In that case, retrying the operation is definitely not the right response :-). So if you use bare `except:`, then maybe your program will accidentally swallow and hide `KeyboardInterrupt`. And if you use `except Exception:` instead, then that only catches exceptions that indicate some error performing the operation, not special weird exceptions like `KeyboardInterrupt`.

But, here we're doing something different! We're not catching an exception and handling it. We're just trying to clean up some resources before exiting. And we want to clean up those resources regardless of why we're exiting – if it's a `KeyboardInterrupt` and the program is shutting down, well, that's fine, we still want to close things. And we aren't going to accidentally hide any exceptions, because this code always re-raises every exception it catches.

So this is one of the specific situations where you really do want to catch *everything*.

Also, as a rule of thumb when making PRs, it's good to avoid making small unrelated changes like this. Maintainers have to read your PR to decide whether to merge it. If there are unrelated changes, it makes it harder to read and figure out if everything is correct. And, if there are unrelated changes, it increases the chance that one of the changes will have some issue, and then that has to be dealt with before the other good changes can go in.

So I would actually argue that we do it (again) just like Git does. Having some local-only options that are not read from the system or user config files because:

* I think Ulf has a point, I don't see why a revision or a URL should be permanently set in a configuration file, thinking about it again for the nth time. This feels like hardcoding the URL you pass to `git clone` or the branch that you pass to `git checkout`.
* Git does this by creating the config options above, we can do something similar so it is clear to the user that those are local-only
* We need to document the global options like Git does here: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config so that the user knows what to expect

So in my view, I would approve this PR if:

1) We save both revision and URL in `west/config`
2) We document the Git options (maybe add a simple one like no colors)
3) We allow `west init` to be re-run after having changed the config file or passing new command-line params to it

For now we need to decrement absolute_timeout in the `finally` block so it's done after every iteration, including ones that end with the `continue` in the `try` block. I'm picking low hanging fruit in this PR. I think fixing everything (e.g. #18552) will require more thought and a larger change.

As for how absolute_timeout can be non-positive here, note that nothing breaks the loop when it is. _configure_timeout raises an exception when absolute_timeout <= 0 but this `except` block handles that exception because it's an AzureError. Good that you point this out. We have to make a decision here. I'm listing the options:

1. Let values outside of the range use the min or max value (current behavior).
  * This makes sense if you have a few outliers you don't want to stretch out your range for.
  * No time to filter out outliers, get stuff done quickly.
2. Raise a UserWarning when values fall outside of the range.
  * Sort of compromise between the current behavior and raising an error.
  * Numpy does this a lot, which results in people figuring out how to disable/ignore them.
  * Kind of annoying (subjective, I know :p )
3. Raise an exception when values fall outside of the range.
  * Having a value lie outside of the range is a mistake and we don't do mistakes.
  * Force the user to give either a good range or remove outliers.
4. Give the values outside the range the same color as `nan_fill_color`.
  * This does show they are not in the range, but it makes them indistinguishable from nan values.
5. Give the values outside the range a special color and add a new argument for it.
  * Makes it easy to see which values fall outside the range.
  * Adds complexity, another argument to check out and set.

I'm currently leaning towards raising an exception. Many users may not pass a custom range, so they will not encounter this issue. If a user passes a custom range, that means he or she is advanced enough to also be able to properly preprocess the data and deal with outliers.

On the other hand I like how folium is hackable. If someone wants to deal with their outliers by cheating a bit, who are we to judge. But then maybe a UserWarning would be appropriate.

So after writing this comment I think I would lean towards leaving it as is and add a UserWarning. Do you want to make the case for another option? I'd like to hear your opinion. We can change the tests based on what we decide.

No, what I mean is that the TVM related logic should be in a single place which infers from our base variables USE_TVM, USE_CUDA and USE_CUDA_PATH. Any variables that are now TVM specific should be constructed in a single block that has "if USE_TVM" as condition. These variables don't leave the scope of that block.

Right now, this introduces more and more TVM specific variables on a global level and scattered across the makefile. Instead it would be good if we have a single block to invoke TVM - this block will take care of converting all existing variables to their TVM counterpart. This improves the modularity and acts as a clear separation between our build system and TVM. It acts like a "translator". The issue to me here is that we are mirroring existing variables we already have at the global scope. Combining this fact with conditional operations on top of these mirrored variables, makes it harder to understand the process flow.

Okay, I can be convinced that `Experiment` isn’t worth its conceptual
weight, and that we should remove it from the public API in favor of
directly writing `hparams_config(hparams, metrics)`.

Instead of complicating the module and dependency structure more by
introducing both `api` and `summary_v2`, I would _like_ to rename `api`
to `summary_v2`, containing:

  - `hparams(hparams)` to log a session start summary;
  - `hparams_config(hparams, metrics)` to log an experiment summary;
  - `HParam`, `Metric`, and domain descriptors, as currently;
  - `KerasCallback`, as currently.

But `KerasCallback` has a hard (module-time) dependency on `tf.keras`,
so this would preclude `summary_v2.hparams` in notf builds, which is not
great. The two options that I see right now are:

 1. omit the callback from `summary_v2` and instead put it in `api`
    (which would need to exist after all), and also let `api` forward
    the other `summary_v2` symbols; or

 2. move the callback into a separate `callback` module.

Option (1) has the downside that I can’t rename `api` to `__init__` as
I’d hoped, because you again wouldn’t be able to import `summary_v2`
without depending on `tf.keras`. Option (2) has the downside of being
ugly.

I guess option (1) is less bad. At least in that case, the vast majority
of users could just use `api` and not worry about `summary_v2` unless
they need a notf build. I’ll implement it and see if I can come up with
anything better.

I saw somewhere you mentioned the idea of having these behave like "wrap" validators. For that, I would think you'd want the signature to be:
```python
    def __modify_pydantic_core_schema__(
        self, source: Type[Any], schema: core_schema.CoreSchema, handler: Callable[[core_schema.CoreSchema], core_schema.CoreSchema]
    ) -> core_schema.CoreSchema:
```

That would let you receive the input schema (as `schema`), make changes before passing it to the other modifiers, pass the modified version into the handler, then make more changes before returning the final result.

More concretely, it might look like:
```python
    def __modify_pydantic_core_schema__(
        self, source: Type[Any], schema: core_schema.CoreSchema, handler: Callable[[core_schema.CoreSchema], core_schema.CoreSchema]
    ) -> core_schema.CoreSchema:
        modified_schema_before_applying_other_modifications = do_before_modifications(schema, source)
        modified_schema_after_applying_other_modifications = handler(modified_schema_before_applying_other_modifications)
        final_schema = do_after_modifications(schema, source)
        return final_schema
```

I would understand if we don't want to go whole-hog on supporting wrapping (though I think it lets you do things you couldn't otherwise do, which is nice), but if we don't do that, it's not clear to me what benefit we get from replacing `schema` with `handler: Callable[[], core_schema.CoreSchema]`.

If the only benefit is that you can choose not to call the handler if you don't need the input value, I am not sure it's worth changing the signature (other than adding `source`, which I think still makes sense), as it doesn't seem to enable much more utility for just a small potential performance improvement. But perhaps I'm misunderstanding the changes here.

If my assessment above is wrong, could you point me at an example of what this change to the signature enables?

    TIME = 0
    ENERGY = 1

    alpha = np.array([e[ENERGY] for e in log], dtype=np.float)

    fdl2 = (fdl - 1) // 2  # Integer division

    ir = np.zeros(int(time_thres * fs) + fdl)

    from pyroomacoustics import build_rir
    build_rir.fast_rir_builder(ir, time, alpha, visibility, fs, fdl)

    if plot :

        plt.figure()
        plt.plot(x, ir)
        plt.title("RIR")
        plt.show()

    return ir
```

We need to put newlines somewhere for text diff to work efficiently, this is one way to put some newlines, but it's different from the status quo. It's been disappointing to find issues with MarshalIndent.

`json.MarhalIndent(x, "", "")` used previously uses two-passes with in-mem buffers, expensive, but produces the desired result of many newlines with no extra space.

`json.NewEncoder(w); enc.SetIndent("", ""); enc.Encode(msg)` is inconsistent with `json.MarhalIndent` as it decides not to include the newlines at all.

Using `enc.SetIndent("", " ")` introduces unwanted whitespace besides the newlines.

`jsoniter` indent options never match the behavior of `json.MarhalIndent(x, "", "")`. It's more like `enc.SetIndent`. It either writes newlines and whitespace, or no newlines at all.

> do we also want setuptools to create a snap7-server binary on install?

We can implement this easily to use the existing script or the new entrypoint:

```python
setup(
    # ...,
    entry_points={
        'console_scripts': [
            'snap7-server = snap7.server.__main__:main',
        ]
    }
)
```

Imagine we're grouping on Series object, its query compiler has `columns = ["__reduced__"]`, so technically we're doing `df.groupby(by="__reduced__")`. There is also a different case that you mentioned when index contains `__reduced__` **value**, which means that this axis is reduced and frame has to be squeezed on that axis at the API layer (`axis=1` - frame is unnamed Series, `axis=0` - frame is transposed Series or scalar). That case does not correlate with this change.

@BowenBao - Thank you! But thinking out loud naively here, let's forget about the corner case that @stevenlix  pointed out for this discussion. The current pad computation logic used by ORT is here - 
https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime/blob/a7039601c4bf469131569d0c1261f13cd6bd67d9/onnxruntime/core/providers/cpu/nn/conv_base.h#L39

This was the previous logic in this same PR - 
`int64_t legacy_target_size = (dim_value + strides[i] - 1) / strides[i];
int64_t total_pad = (legacy_target_size - 1) * strides[i] + kernel_shape[i] - dim_value;`

Please substitute `legacy_target_size` into `total_pad` and simplify by doing some simple math, the result will be (if my math is correct) `kernel_shape[i] - 1` (which is the new logic I proposed). [Here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37674306/what-is-the-difference-between-same-and-valid-padding-in-tf-nn-max-pool-of-t) is another detailed discussion about this and it aligns with the ` kernel_shape[i] - 1`

As long as the total pads computed is the same, it should not affect kernel center position.

Actually, hold on. Sorry, I admit I didn't understand refspecs much until today when I RTFM a bit.

Given `revision: foo` in a manifest file, west expects that `foo` is a SHA, a branch, or a tag.

- if `foo` is a SHA, we are in the previous if block, this line is not executed
- if `foo` is a branch: assuming the remote `.git/foo` and `.git/refs/foo` does not exist, fetching plain `foo` gives us `refs/heads/foo` as desired, and it seems OK to stash that in our local `.git/refs/west/foo`, since we're doing that for SHAs with the `refs/heads/*:refs/heads/*` refspec above
- **but** if `foo` is a tag, say `v1.14.1`, this would create `refs/west/v1.14.1`, which is actually a tag object, created outside of `.git/refs/tags`

Try it:

```
$ git fetch https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr v1.14.0:refs/west/v1.14.0
From https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr
 * [new tag]               v1.14.0    -> refs/west/v1.14.0
$ ls .git/refs/west/v1.14.0
.git/refs/west/v1.14.0
$ git cat-file -t refs/west/v1.14.0
tag
$ git cat-file -t west/v1.14.0
tag
```

But `git tag | grep west` is empty, no results.

Is that really the behavior we want? Serious question, I am really not sure.

Maybe in the case where you're also using a file job store on the shared filesystem, and symlinking everything, because you don't need to take the time to make those symlinks. And in the case where you have a lot of files, because with the file store you're reconstructing the directory structure for all the jobs that want it, instead of sharing it. But I could see cases where using S3 would be faster than bypassing the filestore through a shared filesystem, if your cluster is in AWS and has a pretty slow shared filesystem for home directories or something.

In general I see bypassing the filestore as a big hack to support some weird workflows that need a weird feature, and not really what we want most people to be doing most of the time. It definitely opens up new ways to break things: we don't really have a way for jobs that *don't* have `InplaceUpdateRequirement` to *not* see them, when we do it this way, so jobs might end up seeing files they shouldn't have, and then not work right when run on another cluster where there isn't a shared filesystem and the file store actually has to be used.

Thats _alot_ of text, but I did not want to shirk my duty to explain everything going on. The elevator is important to keep in mind as we move forward with pants and new languages etc:
1. materializing items in the chroot is slow - we get away with not having to internalize this lesson only because we have named caches for the Python stack and these allow us to store single zip files that are only exploded once and centrally to the PEX_ROOT.
2. thrashing the page cache may also be slow - when we store lots of files in LMDB and then materialize in a chroot, they are all as if new files to the OS and must be read in cold.

I'm not sure how much of the 500+ms overhead is due to 1 vs 2, but its simply super expensive to store big dirs in LMDB and then try to use them later to run fine grained things.

This would be a fun problem to work on and we have some ideas including brfs, but for now we can leverage named caches.

>Is there a way we can just always encode the url?

That's what I initially did, but there were [concerns](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/18440#discussion_r704729649) about some pyarrow filesystems doing their own URL-encoding and therefore double-encoding our paths. We could always encode before and decode after, but some filesystems (such as local filesystems or fsspec filesystems) may not like pre-encoded paths.

>If not, is there a way we can avoid checking the string in the exception?

The most specific error type indicating a parsing error is `pa.lib.ArrowInvalid`, and pyarrow appears to rely on this kind of error message substring check internally for certain error types, so no other clearly better route immediately comes to mind. We could copy all of the `_resolve_filesystem_and_path` code and add this exception check directly to the `from_uri()` parsing (as I did earlier in this PR), but we'll still be matching on the error string, so it didn't seem worth it to copy the entire helper function over for logic that could easily wrap the helper. I'm going to open up an Arrow ticket for this, so (IMO) we should consider this a temporary workaround of an Arrow bug and not invest much time in creating a long-term workaround.

@DomFijan Distances are not preserved when shearing, even if Lx, Ly are the same. In the example below, I use a system with two points. I use a square box and a sheared box. The points are the same after wrapping (they are within both boxes). However, the distances between the points under periodic boundary conventions are not the same. In the square system, the distance is `0.707...` while in the sheared case the distance is `1.118...`. I expect this difference in the minimum vectors to affect S(q) but could be convinced otherwise by a test as you proposed.

```python
import freud
import numpy as np
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

box_square = freud.Box.square(3)
box_shear = freud.Box(3, 3, xy=0.5, is2D=True)

points = np.array([[-1.5, -1.5, 0], [-1, 1, 0]])
square_points = box_square.wrap(points)
shear_points = box_shear.wrap(points)

print(box_square)
print(box_shear)

print(points)
print(square_points)
print(shear_points)

print(box_square.compute_all_distances(points, points))
print(box_shear.compute_all_distances(points, points))

system_square = freud.AABBQuery.from_system((box_square, square_points))
system_shear = freud.AABBQuery.from_system((box_shear, shear_points))

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
system_square.plot(ax=ax)
system_shear.plot(ax=ax)
fig.savefig("result.png")
```

The only issue I see with this plan is a potential performance problem. Renderers call the grid's `iter_columns`, on each row, which will force a new call on any columns that have these as lambdas. While that's not a huge deal for a web view with, say, 50-100 rows, that could really bog things down (depending on the efficiency of the lambda) for an XLSX with 100k records.

I think things are fine here, FWIW - the change needs to happen in the renderers. We're not passing row data to these lambdas, so they do not need to be evaluated per row. The renderers need to have a `columns` property that caches its list of columns, and pulls from `iter_columns` when that cache hasn't been set yet.
- I'd like to see a `Renderer` base class with that property
- Some of the generic stuff in the various renderers (`__init__`, `__call__`) can get moved there
- `Renderer.__init__` should check for an `init` method which takes no arguments, and if available, call it. The various renderers can use that to set up anything else they'd need.

There are 3 things I want to highlight regarding this function. 

First from a code point of view, in the same file, the function find_best_threshold_fun_ is a bind for both FindBestThresholdCategorical and FindBestThresholdNumerical so these 2 functions need to have the same template. So I would have to change a significant amount of code to make the 2 not have the same template.

Second, in theory, Say we have a dataset with 2 features, 1 being categorical and 1 numerical, and we apply a monotonic constraint on the numerical one. Then even when when we split the data with respect to the categorical feature, then the best constraints for the resulting leaves may vary depending on which categories you split the categorical feature on. For example if you look at example 11 of the pdf report I posted in my initial comment, then you can imagine that the unconstrained feature is actually a categorical feature, and you see that the constraints applied to the leaves also depend on the categorical splits. Currently this is not implemented in the code, and I don't plan on implementing it in this PR, because it has a pretty marginal use. But I or someone else may, one day, want to implement that and the current way the code works is perfect for that.

Finally, there are some research papers that want to apply monotone constraints to categorical data (e.g. credit ratings, yes-no questions...). So maybe one day someone will want to make a PR implementing monotone constraints for categorical features.

For these reasons, I don't agree with removing the constraints argument from the categorical features splitting function you mentioned. But if you still think that's best in light of the above then I will do it.

Hmm, so `request_queue` should not matter here. We want a given user's bundles to run on their own workers whenever that's possible, regardless of whether `request_queue` is `None` or not on the bundle. In other words, setting `request_queue` doesn't mean scheduling the bundle to only run on your own workers.

What determines whether a run runs on a user-owned worker or public worker is solely whether there's a user-owned worker available at the time we're trying to run that bundle.

As a result, the thing to do here is not sorting the bundles by the `request_queue` parameter. I think the ideal solution would be something like for each staged bundle: 1. get all user owned workers and public workers. 2. sort and filter these workers, and while sorting give first precedence to user-owned workers. 3. In the end, if your top worker is NOT user owned, enforce the parallel run quota.

I think we do need to distinguish between EUI64 and discerner (I think you may be thinking that we can treat EUI64 as a discerner with 64 bit length which would not be the case). 
- In terms of how they are used there are differences: 
   - For EUI64 we use a hash of it to add to bloom filter and also use same as Joiner Id.
   - For Joiner Discerner, there is no hashing for bloom filter (the actual discerner value is directly used for updating bloom filter). For link-local IID (Joiner Id) we currently do add a random prefix to extend it to 64 bit (if not already at 64) and no hashing. We may consider later to update this, e.g., use hash of a random prefix + discerner value to hide the actual discerner value when deriving the link-local IID (note that this would be only for IID derivation and cannnot be used in bloom filter).
- Also in terms of representation (in `JoinerInfo`),  the discerner is an unsigned int `uint64_t` value so its byte order is machine/platform dependent (little/big endian), compared to `EUI64` which is a MAC Ext Address (byte order is fixed).

I should also add that, in general `self.downloaded` is unreliable, because it is computed inconsistently, sometimes
as
```
self.downloaded += len(chunk)
```
and sometimes
```
self.downloaded += self._block_size
```

Both of them can be wrong, in general: requests configures urllib3 to do decoding based on the content-encoding header of the response (e.g. gunzip the data stream). It tries to read block_size bytes, then decodes them. However, for chunked http responses, the block_size is not actually used; instead, the chunks are iterated over.

So in essence, there is no way to reliably know how many of the original bytes were downloaded unless it's clear there is the special case of no http response chunking and no content decoding. This cannot be assumed at this point, as this is a general-purpose library.

I think @klywang's chosen language (inside/outside) is unambiguous here. The box has finite volume and defined boundaries, even though periodic boundary conditions are intended to behave as though the box is infinite. @klywang @tommy-waltmann I have some background info below on topics related to this that may be of interest to you and future developers.

freud assumes periodic boundary conditions in virtually all methods. This is an assumption that we often want to relax, in cases like @klywang has here, creating a virtual box that is a subset of a larger box and can be used to "crop." We have long-standing issues (#209, #573) from users in our group and external users where explicit support for non-periodic boxes would be helpful. We have flags for tracking box periodicity that are almost never used internally, and this is not the desired behavior.

To Tommy's question: whether a set of input points "obeys periodic boundary conditions" is not always well-defined, and it depends largely on what users are trying to achieve. Consider cases like polymers where polymer beads may be wrapped such that the molecule is contiguous and its center of mass is inside the periodic box, even though some of its components are outside the box. In such a case, does the molecule "obey" periodic boundary conditions?

Another subtlety here that @klywang got right, but I wanted to echo the motivation -- we try to use "points" instead of "particles" in all docstrings (wherever appropriate) because many tools in the freud library are fairly generic and have some general applicability outside the space of nanoparticle self-assembly. Particles could be atoms (much smaller scale), but they could also be points in a geometry sense, or even data points in a 2D/3D vector space that obeys periodic boundary conditions (I have used freud for such a thing). According to a post on our forum from a while back, we have even had users leverage the library for analyzing crowd dynamics.

> @jameslamb there's one caveat with _choose_param_value, when objective is string and fobj callable, the second one should take precedence

I see you're referring to the code below, where if `fobj` is not `None`, it is preferred to anything found in `params`.

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/8e721c564d4534820a7b7febf81382ccf247b5c9/python-package/lightgbm/engine.py#L127-L131

I personally support making the breaking change in this PR that if `fobj` is not `None` and if a value for `objective` or its aliases is found in `params`, the value from `params` is preferred. I think that across all places in the Python and R packages, LightGBM should try to provide the behavior "values provided via `params` override keyword arguments".

Let's get others' opinions before making changes though.

-------


@StrikerRUS @shiyu1994 @guolinke  what do you think? Given the following:

```python
lgb.train(
    params={"objective": _other_custom_objective_function},
    fobj=_some_custom_objective_function,
    ...
)
```

- **option 1**: use the function passed to `fobj` (current behavior)
- **option 2**: use the function passed to `params` (breaking change)
- **option 3**: raise an error about conflicting objectives (breaking change)
- **option 4**: something else

My preference is for **option 2**, for consistency with how `params` is used in most other places in the Python and R packages.

you've probably passed value of the enum to the `source_type` enum on postgres side:

```python
    source_type = Column(
        ENUM(
            ThothAdviserIntegrationEnum.CLI.value,
            ThothAdviserIntegrationEnum.KEBECHET.value,

            name="source_type",
            create_type=True,
        ),
        nullable=True,
    )

That will turn name into an `int` based on `auto()` invocation. You can provide enum names (using this trick should not enforce their enumeration every time):

```python
    source_type = Column(

        ),

Coupla points. 
1. There's an implicit legacy issue in https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/6b356a4eacde1280f78b4ed966e8a499a727189a/rdflib/graph.py#L641 where `Graph.__add__()` accumulates namespace bindings which `Graph.__sub__()` ignores.
2. I'll copy [gromgull's observations](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/issues/543) here, for convenience:
> The confusion here is the two arguments `replace` and `override`
> 
> Say they release FOAF 2.0 (Now based on google+? :) ) and you want to change `foaf` to point to `http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0/`. Up to now you had `foaf=>http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1` and `foaf2=>http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0`
> 
> Now you want to "upgrade" to FOAF 2.0 :
> 
> ```py
> g.namespace_manager.bind('foaf', Namespace('http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0'), .... )
> ```
> 
> Adding:
> 
>     `override` will do what you want, and replace the current binding for foaf to be the new URI.
> 
>     `replace` will replace *any other bindings pointing to the same namespace*, so the 
>      old foaf2 binding will be removed.
> 
> In the FOAF2 example, you probably want both.

and [further](https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/issues/543#issuecomment-158754466)

> The problem is (maybe) in the individual implementation, for instance, here:
> 
> https://github.com/RDFLib/rdflib/blob/master/rdflib/plugins/memory.py#L150
> 
> that when replacing, the `uri=>prefix` is updated correctly (i.e. the 
> http://xmlns.com/foaf/2.0 now points to `foaf3`) but the `prefix=>uri` map of course 
> has both. This should be cleaned up if the prefix was already used.
> 
> Also, an unbind method would be handy. Sometimes you want to remove a prefix without providing a new one.

```
    # In the same batch during inference, it is possible that some translations have target prefix
    # while others do not have. It is also possible that translation may have a target prefix with
    # different length to others. Thus prefix ids may include a full zero vector if a translation
    # in the batch does not have prefix, or include a vector padding with zeros on the right if some
    # translations are with shorter prefix. An example of prefix ids reflecting length differences \
    # is as follows:
    #
    # [1, 2, 3]
    # [1, 2, 0]
    # [0, 0, 0]
    #
    # Here, the first sentence has a prefix of length 3, the second one has a prefix of length 1 \
    # and the last one does not have prefix.
    #
    # At any timestep, some target prefix ids could be zero. If a prefix id is zero for a translation \
    # at a timestep, all hots in the vocab are assigned to 1 (instead of only one hot is assigned to 1). \
    # This makes sure there is no constraint on selecting any specific target token for the translation
    # in that case.

    prefix_in_one_hot.masked_fill_(prefix.unsqueeze(-1) == 0, 1)
```

The problem I see here is that the `modin_series` object is the same all over the parameters. ```python # Different objects for every element in the 'by' list @pytest.mark.parametrize("by", [[pd.Series([..]), pd.Series([..])]]) # Same objects for every element in the 'by' list @pytest.mark.parametrize("by", [[modin_series, modin_series]]) ``` Currently, this doesn't cause any problems, but potentially could. I see several ways to avoid this way of initialization, none of them are perfect though: 1. Default-to-pandas warning is raised in the Series constructor for `BaseOnPython` execution only. Since we already mark all of the tests for `BaseOnPython` to ignore default-to-pandas warnings, why no to do this in the `conftest.py`. However, this still won't solve the problem if some of the non-`BaseOnPython` execution would start raising default to pandas in the frame's construction someday. https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/72ca672f3e79cb4cdd589692db2d0e19d696da5c/modin/conftest.py#L266-L267 ```python if execution == BASE_EXECUTION_NAME: set_base_execution(BASE_EXECUTION_NAME) # Change 'filterwarnings' attribute directly in the pytest's config config.addinivalue_line("filterwarnings", "default:.*defaulting to pandas.*:UserWarning") ``` 2. Replace explicit Series construction with the Series' getters, which would construct a new Series inside the test function, which shouldn't cause any problems. ```python @pytest.mark.parametrie("by", [lambda: [pd.Series([..]), pd.Series([...])]]) def test_fn(by): # Create Series inside the test function affected by the global pytestmark by = by() ... ``` As I said, I'm not pushing to do these changes in this PR, let's wait for other reviewers' opinions.

1) Use polymorphism. This removes if-else statements but requires a lot more code and perhaps not so Pythonic here.
2) Create DFA class. This for-loop simulates a finite automata, so I can create a separate FA class and this will hide most of the if-else complexity. But, it requires quite a bit of careful formulation and thought to correctly create states and transitions.
3) What Vadym suggested above (e.g. `dict`). I don't think this improves readability as I either need to nest if-statements or create private functions, which IMO introduces indirection here.

Out of 3 options, the 2nd (DFA) is the best IMO, but it would take me more time to complete, putting our deadlines at risk.

After hacking on the batched/grouped `pytest` implementation, I find myself not loving the schema of this type:
* Most plugins will (or at least currently) run with a single field-set per partition, so you end up with a _lot_ of `asserts` on `len(field_sets)` at the beginning of plugin rules to ensure only 1 thing is passed in. Feels like something the type system should be checking for us.
* In the `pytest` case I find myself writing a decent amount of `partition.field_sets[0]` to get at fields like `extra_env_vars`, with the invariant that every `field_set` in the partition has the same values for those fields. Again feels like something I wish I could model in the type system by defining a new `class` with a `tuple[Address, ...]` and then non-tuple fields for values that should be constant within a partition.

I am considering this refactor:
* Introduce a new generic type var for `_PartitionElementT`
* Change `Partitions` to `Partitions(FrozenDict[Any, _PartitionElementT])`
* Change `SubPartition` to `SubPartition(Generic[_PartitionElementT])`

Then for plugins that test one-file-per-process, `_PartitionElementT` = `PluginTestFieldSet`. But in the `pytest` case I can create whatever class I want to conveniently model the metadata.

Yes, good point. So, the queries I converted to inner joins above are all OK in this regard; I verified each of them individually. Basically, the subqueries (aside from the one you highlighted in a comment above and one other which I didn’t convert) are used as conjuncts in the final WHERE clause, aren’t negated, and require the existence of something in the table in the subquery to be true. In other words, each of those subqueries is automatically false if the table is empty anyway.

This is _not_ true for the two other queries I didn't convert earlier (the one used for .floating and one used when the querying user is not root). For those, an inner join would not work for the reason you mentioned. For the negated query, it checks that the bundle_uuid is NOT in the Worksheet_Item table, and so using an INNER JOIN would be incorrect since the inner join would be empty even though the result we _really_ want is for every bundle_uuid to be in the result since none belong to a worksheet (since worksheet_item is empty). For this query (and for the other I mentioned), we remedy this by using a LEFT OUTER JOIN.

I verified that my changes work by adding test cases. I added some for .floating, and I added some cases for cl search queries used by the non-root user. I verified these work and that, as expected, they all fail when I switch the LEFT OUTER JOIN to an INNER JOIN.

The AGPL license however (as a copyleft license) also doesn't protect the author's work from copying, modifying, sharing. It establishes a non-dispensable mandatory freedom that everybody who uses that code must share with others and publish also all his/her code under the same copyleft license and give other users the same right on his work, even to end users who do not need a software and only use a service by the network. "Network use is distribution" is a principle of AGPL. Copyleft makes a strong border between "free" open-source world and commercial world. By blocking you mean when `future.result()` is called?

The code that calls `future = subscriber.subscribe()` does not have to call `.result()` on it, it can do other things while callbacks are processing received messages. The code might pass a reference to this future to, say, a "manager" thread that cancels the future after a certain time period, and might want to be notified when this happens to do some finalization work. Having an option to register an `on_done_callback` is ideal for that.

Generally, if something is not used, it should not be simply hidden in the docs, but instead removed altogether IMO. However, I can imagine `on_done_callback` being useful in some applications ...

I strongly suggest that this algorithm be updated to allow more precise calculations when the total broadband "sun" irradiance is known. This covers, for example, the case for primary reference cell calibrations at NREL where a cavity radiometer provides the total irradiance alongside a spectral radiometer with a smaller response window. (This is where the discrepancy was previously discovered as a significant source of error. Admittedly, this level of error may not be important for other types of workflows.)

Here is a suggestion on how to address this using two changes:

1) In the function `get_am15g`, scale this spectrum from ASTM G173-03 so that it matches the broadband spectrum in IEC  60904-3 (2008), in which the tail irradiance is fully specified and the full curve (to lambda=infinity) integrates to precisely 1000 W/m^2. Namely, change this line in `get_am15g`
```python
am15g = pd.read_csv(filepath, index_col=0, squeeze=True)
```
to
```python
    am15g_raw = pd.read_csv(filepath, index_col=0, squeeze=True)
    am15g_raw_integral_to_4_micron = np.trapz(am15g_raw, x=am15g_raw.index, axis=-1)  # W/m^2
    iec_integral_to_4_micron = 997.47  # W/m^2, from IEC 60904-3 (2008)
    am15g = iec_integral_to_4_micron / am15g_raw_integral_to_4_micron * am15g_raw
```

2) Pass optional total irradiance parameters to the function `calc_spectral_mismatch`, namely
```python
def calc_spectral_mismatch(sr, e_sun, e_sun_tot=None, e_ref=None, e_ref_tot=1000):
```
and then add the following logic to `calc_spectral_mismatch` before the usable fraction calculations:
```python
    if e_sun_tot is None:
        # Use integrated spectral irradiance, typically an approximation
        # that misses "tail" of distribution.
        e_sun_tot = integrate(e_sun)

    if e_ref_tot is None:
        # Use integrated spectral irradiance, typically an approximation
        # that misses "tail" of distribution.
        e_ref_tot = integrate(e_ref)

    # calculate usable fractions
    uf_sun = integrate(e_sun * sr_sun) / e_sun_tot
    uf_ref = integrate(e_ref * sr_ref) / e_ref_tot
```

I have an example that shows an error on the order of 0.25% in the spectral correction factor from ignoring the integral of the tail. This well approximates the degree of error that I recall discovering at NREL. I am happy to share this out separately but am omitting here for brevity.

I have also reached out to Carl Osterwald about the scaling and "tail" discrepancies between ASTM G173-03 and IEC 60904-3 (2008), which seems to persist.

Finally, I still have some questions about the numerical effect of partitioning on the integrations for coarse SR functions, but I would like to settle the above first before further investigation.

I just mean support the minimal api, which is allowing the user to pass environment variables via **extra_vars. If code that call this wants to support a json encoding of the env vars that code can decode the json to a dict to pass in as **extra_vars.

Concreteley, just:
```python
def _bootstrap(entry_point, include_pexrc=False, **extra_vars):
  from .pex_info import PexInfo
  pex_info = PexInfo.from_pex(entry_point)
  env = dict(PEX_IGNORE_RCFILES=str(not include_pexrc), **extra_vars)
  pex_info.update(PexInfo.from_env(env))
```

Calling code in an application can do this if it want to unburdening Pex from supporting json in this API in perpetuity:
```python
_bootstrap(entry_point, **json.loads(extra_vars_json_string))
```

 My basic understanding is that SVD (and other matrix factorizations) are usually differentiable but sometimes are numerically unstable. I'm not sure exactly how this maps to specifying differentiability in the onnx operator inputs/outputs -- should all input and output differentiability be unknown?
From the pytorch docs re: SVD differentiability
Gradients computed using U or Vh will only be finite when A does not have repeated singular values. If A is rectangular, additionally, zero must also not be one of its singular values. Furthermore, if the distance between any two singular values is close to zero, the gradient will be numerically unstable ...
torch.linalg.svdvals() computes only the singular values. Unlike torch.linalg.svd(), the gradients of svdvals() are always numerically stable.
https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.linalg.svd.html#torch.linalg.svd
https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.linalg.svdvals.html

In a previous version I checked the height and width diff individually before sampling and just setting `0` if there is no diff. This is a speed-up since in this case the call looks like `int(torch.randint(0, 1, size=()))` which is always `0`.

However, checking individually is different from what we do now in prototype and in stable:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/2d92728341bbd3dc1e0f1e86c6a436049bbb3403/torchvision/transforms/transforms.py#L634-L639

Thus, this could be regarded as BC breaking change since we would change the sampling in some cases. For example, this triggered our consistency tests since there we don't have a height diff, but call `torch.randint` twice since there is a width diff. If we check separately, we only call `torch.randint` once and thus get a different parameter set even if we set the random seed before the call.

```suggestion
With Java servers, to find the server, we sometimes end up performing an SRV DNS 
lookup. This means making a request to your DNS server and waiting for an answer,
making that lookup a blocking operation (during which other things can be done).

However with Bedrock servers, no such lookups are required (bedrock doesn't 
support SRV records), and so there is no blocking I/O operation being made, that
would justify having an async version. 

In fact, all that the bedrock lookup does is parsing the ``host:port`` address, and 
obtaining the ``host`` and ``port`` parts out of it (with some error handling, and 
support for default ports).
```
Might be worth linking to some docs explaining what SRV records are. Alternatively, we could also just have a tip/note block with a quick explanation, like this one:

```
An SRV record allows the server to have an address like: `hypixel.net`, that points to
a some specified IP/Host and port, depending on this record.

That way, even if the server is hosted on a non-standard port (other than 25565, say 8855), 
you won't need to use `myserver.com:8855`, since the port number will simply be stored
in the SRV record, so people can still connect simply with `myserver.com`.

On top of that, it also allows to specify a different IP/Host, which means you don't need to
use the same server to run both the website, and the minecraft server. Instead, the SRV
record can simply point to a different IP/Host address (like `mc.hypixel.net`, or `209.222.114.112`).
```

The original `.split` method is already implemented in the manner of not extracting the metadata:
https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/cd7611cd6d7fd4054e06017d879234261293561b/modin/core/dataframe/pandas/partitioning/partition.py#L394-L398
So the full-axis implementation just follows the initial approach.

We don't want to extract metadata because:
1. Partitions generated by this function are temporary, at the reshuffling flow the `split_row_partitions` are immediately replaced by `new_partitions` holding new metadata, meaning that the metadata of `split_row_partitions` is never accessed:
   https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/cd7611cd6d7fd4054e06017d879234261293561b/modin/core/dataframe/pandas/partitioning/partition_manager.py#L1600-L1608
2. The splitting stage generates a lot of partitions (up to `ncores ^ 2`), it's already not an easy task for ray to put into storage that big amount of futures at once, the situation becomes even worse when we ask to store the metadata futures as well (`4 * (ncores ^ 2)` amount of futures at once). I've measured the case from #5533 with and without the partition's metadata, and received a stable 9% speed-up (~ 0.12s) for the case without metadata.

It does end up as an error, but the error handling happens later on. The structure of the code, before this PR, is along the lines of:

1. Go through the schema, applying renamings via the visitor pattern
2. If there are any unused renamings, then raise an error saying which renamings were unused.

Now, we're in a bit of a catch-22 situation: the point of the visitor is that we don't have access to the entire schema-- only individual nodes at any one time. However, we [can't find out which interface nodes to be made unqueryable without](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/issues/124) `schema_ast.definitions`, which needs to be done outside a visitor object (and therefore before any detection of unused renamings).

The workaround that this PR currently uses is basically ignoring unused type renamings because they'll be handled later on in the helper functions that use visitor objects. Hacky, but I'm not sure if we have other options here.

## Problem statement ##
If the structure of a graph is still the same all the time (apart of fusing). Then we can try to avoid running DFS algorithm multiple times.  For brevity, those calls force us to pay a penalty: O(V + E). If we assume that `n` represents a total number of patterns, then the summary cost of that is equal n * O(V +E). Even more, for a few operators (i.e) DFS might be run twice or more, if any ~ then we got mn * O(V +E). 

## Solution - short description ##
Reduce overall time of using DFS to a minimum: nice to have just O(V +E), by labelling nodes and keeping traversal order. Pattern matching is applied after that ~ on a traversal order vector (maybe LCA knowledge is needed).

Previously, version was not used in constructing a `DeploymentReplica` instance, and is instead populated in `check_ready()` when `ready_obj_ref` returns with metadata including the version. 
Now, version is always passed in when constructing a `DeploymentReplica` instance. This will be an accurate version when we are starting a new replica (i.e. `replica = DeploymentReplica(version), replica.start()`), but it may be inaccurate when we are recovering a replica (i.e. `replica = DeploymentReplica(version), replica.recover()`). However, we can guarantee that version will never be None in a DeploymentReplica instance.

Also, the target state of a deployment should never have a null version (unless the target state hasn't been set yet, in which case nothing is starting replicas).

Can we have the same API for buffered and non-buffered encoders? We could avoid any potential confusions by settling on a change to the API across encoders. We could replace `encode_trace` and `encode_traces` with `put`  (as you've done here) and include another function (`encode` in your new buffered encoder) that returns the object for the writer to act upon. `import scrapy

class AwesomeSpider(scrapy.Spider):

    def start_requests(self):
        # GET request

            url="https://httpbin.org/post",

sample-debian-package

├── empty
└── etc
    └── configs

Thinking more about it.  I feel may be we must just raise an exception when line doesn't have sufficient parts as per the specification.  Reason why I think raising an exception is better than silently ignoring invalid packets:

1. Issue #127 is generally not an issue for a well behaved clients
2. This is however a possible attack vector because malicious users/hackers can start sending specially crafted request to the server.
3. If we choose to ignore invalid bytes, a hacker can still overwhelm the server by streaming invalid chunks to the server.
4. E.g. imagine, someone by mistake connects a SSH client on port `8899`.  What will happen then?  Will our parser keep on rejecting invalid bytes, expecting to see a valid HTTP packet someday?   May be answer is to drop such invalid clients as soon as possible.

I think we can simply raise a `NotImplementedError` for now which will eventually result in dropping the client connection from the core.   Wdyt?
